front: What are the two device roles in a client–server network?
back: Clients request resources; servers provide them. The roles describe behavior, not hardware.

front: LAN vs WAN in one sentence each?
back: LAN: one site, you own it, high speed. WAN: connects sites over distance, usually leased from a provider.

front: Which device forwards frames using MAC addresses?
back: A switch (Layer 2).

front: Which device routes packets between networks using IP addresses?
back: A router (Layer 3).

front: Links needed for a full mesh of n devices?
The three layers of Cisco's campus design?
back: Access (hosts plug in), Distribution (aggregation + policy), Core (fast backbone).

front: Data-center architecture where every leaf connects to every spine?
back: Spine-leaf — any server is two hops from any other; built for east-west traffic.

front: Maximum distance for copper UTP Ethernet?
back: 100 meters per segment.

front: Multimode vs single-mode fiber — typical use?
back: Multimode: inside buildings (~550 m). Single-mode: between buildings/cities (km+).

front: Which feature auto-corrects a wrong (straight vs crossover) cable choice?
back: Auto-MDIX.

front: Name OSI layers 1–7 in order.
back: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application.

front: PDU names at L4, L3, L2, L1?
back: Segment, Packet, Frame, Bits.

front: Which addresses change per router hop, and which stay end-to-end?
back: MAC addresses change each hop (frame rebuilt); IP addresses stay constant (unless NAT).

front: The 4 layers of the TCP/IP model?
back: Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access.

front: The three addresses in every conversation and their scopes?
back: MAC (L2, local link), IP (L3, end-to-end), Port (L4, application on the device).

front: How long is a MAC address and how is it written?
back: 48 bits — 12 hex digits, e.g. 3C:52:82:4F:9A:D1. First half = OUI (vendor).

front: Ethernet broadcast destination MAC?
back: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF

front: Default Ethernet MTU?
back: 1500 bytes of payload.

front: What does the FCS in an Ethernet frame do?
back: CRC error DETECTION — corrupted frames are silently dropped (no correction).

front: What does ARP do?
back: Resolves a known IP address to its MAC address — broadcast request, unicast reply, cached in the ARP table.

front: Destination on another subnet — whose MAC does the sender ARP for?
back: The default gateway's MAC.

front: The three actions a switch can take with a frame?
back: Learn (source MAC→port), Forward (known unicast), Flood (unknown unicast/broadcast, all ports except arrival).

front: Collision domains and broadcast domains on a 24-port switch, one VLAN?
back: 24 collision domains (one per port), 1 broadcast domain.

front: What is a VLAN, in domain terms?
back: One broadcast domain — logically a separate switch. Inter-VLAN traffic needs a Layer 3 device.

front: Access port vs trunk port?
back: Access: one VLAN, untagged, for hosts. Trunk: many VLANs, 802.1Q-tagged, between switches.

front: What does 802.1Q add to a frame and how big is it?
back: A 4-byte VLAN tag (12-bit VLAN ID → 4094 VLANs) inserted after the source MAC.

front: Which VLAN crosses a trunk untagged?
back: The native VLAN (default VLAN 1) — both trunk ends must agree on it.

front: ROAS subinterface config — the two key commands?
back: encapsulation dot1q <vlan-id> + ip address <gateway> <mask> on each subinterface.

front: What is an SVI?
back: Switched Virtual Interface — 'interface vlan X' on an L3 switch, acting as that VLAN's default gateway.

front: How is the STP root bridge chosen?
back: Lowest bridge ID: lowest priority first (default 32768), lowest MAC breaks ties.

front: Classic STP port states in order?
back: Blocking → Listening → Learning → Forwarding (~30–50 s total; RSTP does it in seconds).

front: PortFast + BPDU Guard — what do they do?
back: PortFast: host ports skip to forwarding. BPDU Guard: err-disable the port if a BPDU (rogue switch) appears.

front: LACP modes and which combos form a channel?
back: active (initiates) / passive (responds). active+active ✓, active+passive ✓, passive+passive ✗.

front: PAgP modes?
back: desirable (initiates) / auto (responds) — Cisco-proprietary. desirable+desirable or desirable+auto form.

front: The RFC 1918 private ranges?
A host shows 169.254.x.x — what happened?
back: APIPA self-assignment: the host couldn't reach a DHCP server.
IPv4 loopback — 'this machine'; tests the local IP stack.

front: Usable hosts formula for a subnet?
back: 2^(host bits) − 2 (minus network and broadcast addresses).

front: /26 in dotted decimal, block size, usable hosts?
back: 255.255.255.192, block 64, 62 usable hosts.

front: The 'magic number' shortcut?
back: 256 − interesting mask octet = block size; subnets step by that value. Network = multiple ≤ address.

front: /30 vs /31 on point-to-point links?
back: /30: 4 addresses, 2 usable. /31: 2 addresses, both usable (modern point-to-point standard).

front: The VLSM golden rule?
back: Allocate the LARGEST subnets first — big blocks need aligned boundaries.

front: How long is an IPv6 address and how is it written?
back: 128 bits — 8 groups of 4 hex digits; shorten with leading-zero removal and ONE ::

front: IPv6 prefixes: global unicast, link-local, unique local, multicast?
What is SLAAC?
back: Stateless autoconfig: host combines the RA-advertised /64 prefix with its own interface ID — no DHCP needed.

front: IPv6's replacement for ARP?
back: NDP — Neighbor Solicitation/Advertisement over ICMPv6 multicast.

front: EUI-64 in 3 steps?
back: Split MAC in half, insert FFFE, flip the 7th bit of the first byte.

front: Route selection order?
back: 1) Longest prefix match, 2) lowest administrative distance, 3) lowest metric.

front: AD values: connected, static, eBGP, EIGRP, OSPF, RIP?
In [110/2] from show ip route, what are 110 and 2?
back: 110 = administrative distance (OSPF), 2 = the route's metric (cost).

front: Command for an IPv4 default static route?
back: ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>

front: What is a floating static route?
back: A static route with artificially high AD that stays hidden until the primary route disappears — a backup.

front: What must MATCH for OSPF neighbors to form?
back: Area ID, subnet, hello/dead timers, authentication. (Router IDs must be UNIQUE.)

front: OSPF metric formula?
back: Cost = reference bandwidth ÷ interface bandwidth, summed along the path.

front: OSPF DR election rules?
back: Highest interface priority wins (default 1, 0 = never); tie → highest router ID. No preemption.

front: OSPF hello multicast address and default timers?
back: 224.0.0.5; hello 10 s / dead 40 s on Ethernet.

front: What is OSPF Area 0?
back: The backbone — every other area must connect to it (via ABRs).

front: HSRP: what do hosts use as gateway, and what's the failover trick?
back: A virtual IP + virtual MAC (0000.0c07.acXX). Standby takes over the virtual identity when Active dies.

front: HSRP preemption default?
back: OFF — a recovered higher-priority router won't reclaim Active without 'standby preempt'.

front: Which FHRP load-balances per host?
back: GLBP (Cisco) — the AVG hands different virtual MACs to different hosts.

front: TCP three-way handshake?
back: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK.

front: What provides TCP flow control?
back: The sliding window — the receiver advertises how much it can accept before the sender must pause.

front: Why does VoIP use UDP?
back: Retransmitted audio arrives too late to matter — dropping beats delaying for real-time media.

front: Ports: HTTP, HTTPS, SSH, Telnet, DNS?
back: 80, 443, 22, 23 (all TCP); DNS 53 (UDP; TCP for zone transfers).

front: Ports: DHCP, NTP, SNMP, Syslog, TFTP?
back: DHCP UDP 67/68, NTP UDP 123, SNMP UDP 161 (traps 162), Syslog UDP 514, TFTP UDP 69.

front: Ports: FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP?
back: FTP TCP 20/21, SMTP TCP 25, POP3 TCP 110, IMAP TCP 143.

front: DNS record types: A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, PTR?
back: A: name→IPv4. AAAA: name→IPv6. CNAME: alias. MX: mail server. PTR: IP→name (reverse).

front: The DHCP DORA sequence?
back: Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge — first two broadcast from client side, lease begins at ACK.

front: Command that relays DHCP broadcasts to a remote server?
back: ip helper-address <server-ip> — on the router interface FACING THE CLIENTS.

front: NAT terms: inside local vs inside global?
back: Inside local = host's private IP; inside global = the same host's public, translated IP.

front: Which NAT type shares ONE public IP among many hosts, and how?
back: PAT (overload) — unique translated source ports distinguish each session.

front: The 4 ingredients of a PAT config?
back: ip nat inside (LAN if), ip nat outside (WAN if), an ACL selecting sources, 'ip nat inside source list X interface Y overload'.

front: Syslog severity levels 0–7?
back: 0 Emergency, 1 Alert, 2 Critical, 3 Error, 4 Warning, 5 Notification, 6 Informational, 7 Debugging.

front: 'logging trap 4' sends which severities?
back: 0 through 4 (warnings and worse) — the level is an inclusive ceiling counting toward zero.

front: Which SNMP version is secure, and what is NTP stratum?
back: SNMPv3 (auth + encryption). Stratum = distance in hops from the reference clock (stratum 0).

front: QoS: delay, jitter, loss targets for voice?
back: ≤150 ms one-way delay, ≤30 ms jitter, ≤1% loss.

front: Policing vs shaping?
back: Policing DROPS (or re-marks) excess traffic; shaping BUFFERS and delays it to a target rate.

front: Standard voice marking and queue?
back: DSCP EF (46) in a low-latency priority queue (LLQ) — voice always transmits first.

front: The CIA triad?
back: Confidentiality (only authorized access), Integrity (unaltered data), Availability (systems usable).

front: Phishing vs spear phishing vs whaling?
back: Phishing: mass emails. Spear phishing: targeted at a person/org. Whaling: targeted at executives.

front: RADIUS vs TACACS+ — the three contrasts?
back: RADIUS: UDP 1812/1813, encrypts only the password, network access. TACACS+: TCP 49, encrypts whole payload, device admin with per-command authz.

front: Prerequisites for enabling SSH on IOS?
back: hostname + ip domain name → crypto key generate rsa → local user → 'transport input ssh' on VTYs.

front: The two unbreakable ACL laws?
back: 1) Top-down, first match wins. 2) Implicit deny any at the end.

front: Standard vs extended ACL placement?
back: Standard (source-only): near the DESTINATION. Extended (precise): near the SOURCE.

front: Port-security violation modes?
back: shutdown = err-disable (default); restrict = drop + log + count; protect = drop silently.

front: What do DHCP snooping and DAI protect against?
back: Snooping blocks rogue DHCP servers (untrusted ports) and builds a binding table; DAI uses it to stop ARP spoofing.

front: IPsec's four security services?
back: Confidentiality (encryption), integrity (hashing), authentication (peers), anti-replay (sequence numbers).

front: Why GRE over IPsec?
back: Plain IPsec can't carry multicast (routing protocols). GRE encapsulates anything; IPsec encrypts the GRE tunnel.

front: Non-overlapping 2.4 GHz channels?
How does Wi-Fi share the medium?
back: CSMA/CA — listen, random backoff, transmit, require an ACK. Half-duplex, shared airtime.

front: Lightweight AP ↔ WLC protocol and ports?
back: CAPWAP: UDP 5246 (control) and 5247 (data). AP port = access; WLC port = trunk.

front: WPA2 vs WPA3 in one line each?
back: WPA2: AES-CCMP with the 4-way PSK handshake. WPA3: SAE handshake, forward secrecy, kills offline dictionary attacks.

front: Type 1 vs Type 2 hypervisor?
back: Type 1 runs on bare metal (ESXi, KVM); Type 2 runs as an app on a host OS (VirtualBox).

front: Containers vs VMs?
back: Containers share the host kernel (MBs, ms startup); VMs each carry a full guest OS (GBs, minutes).

front: IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS?
back: IaaS: rent infrastructure (EC2). PaaS: rent a platform, bring code. SaaS: rent finished software (M365).

front: What is SD-WAN's core capability?
back: Per-application path selection across multiple transports (MPLS/broadband/LTE) with centralized policy and health probes.

front: Control plane vs data plane vs management plane?
back: Control: builds tables (OSPF, STP). Data: forwards packets using them. Management: how you configure (SSH, SNMP, APIs).

front: Northbound vs southbound APIs?
back: Northbound: apps → controller (REST). Southbound: controller → devices (NETCONF, RESTCONF, OpenFlow).

front: HTTP verbs → CRUD?
back: POST=Create, GET=Read, PUT/PATCH=Update, DELETE=Delete.

front: Three things that make JSON invalid?
back: Single quotes, trailing commas, unquoted keys.

front: Ansible in four attributes?
back: Agentless, SSH/API push, YAML playbooks, idempotent.

front: Terraform in one line?
back: Declarative infrastructure-as-code: describe the end state, plan the diff, apply, track in a state file.

front: Predictive vs generative AI in networking?
back: Predictive: forecasts/detects from telemetry (capacity, anomalies). Generative: creates content (configs, summaries) — always review before deploying.
